sections.title: Concession: -기는 하다
sections.explanation: -기는 하다 acknowledges something is true while implying a 'but' — a concession pattern. The verb is nominalized with -기, followed by the topic particle 는, then 하다. It means 'it IS [verb], but...' For example: 맛있기는 해요. 근데 너무 비싸요 (It IS delicious, but it's too expensive). 알기는 알아요 (I DO know, but...). In casual speech, -기는 often contracts to -긴: 맛있긴 해요 (It IS tasty, but...). 좋긴 좋은데... (It IS good, but...). This pattern is used when you want to partially agree before introducing a contrasting point. The repeated verb form (알기는 알다, 가기는 가다) adds emphasis: 'I do indeed [verb], however...'
sections.tip: The contracted form -긴 하다 is much more common in spoken Korean. 맛있긴 한데 비싸요 is natural; 맛있기는 하는데 비싸요 is correct but more formal. Both mean the same thing.

examples.en: Korean IS difficult, but it's fun
I DID eat, but I'm still hungry
It IS expensive, but the quality is good
I DO know, but it's hard to explain

options: It's not pretty and it's expensive
It IS pretty, but it's too expensive
exercises.explanation: -긴 하다 (contracted from -기는 하다) concedes that it is pretty, then -ㄴ데 introduces the contrasting point: but it's too expensive.

exercises.prompt: What is the casual contracted form of 재미있기는 해요?
exercises.explanation: -기는 contracts to -긴 in casual speech: 재미있기는 → 재미있긴. The rest stays the same.

options: When you strongly disagree with something
When you partially agree but want to add a contrasting point
When you fully agree with no reservations
exercises.explanation: -기는 하다 is for concession — you acknowledge something is true (partially agree) before introducing a 'but.' It shows nuanced, balanced thinking.

sections.title: Near Miss: -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다
sections.explanation: -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다 means 'almost did' or 'nearly happened' — expressing a close call where something was about to happen but ultimately did not. The verb stem takes -(으)ㄹ (future modifier), then 뻔하다. It is almost always used in past tense: -(으)ㄹ 뻔했다. 넘어질 뻔했어요 (I almost fell). 늦을 뻔했어요 (I almost was late). 죽을 뻔했어요 (I almost died — often used as dramatic exaggeration). 잊어버릴 뻔했어요 (I almost forgot). This pattern always implies the event did NOT actually happen — it was narrowly avoided. It often conveys relief (for bad things avoided) or regret (for good things missed): 합격할 뻔했어요 (I almost passed — but didn't).
sections.tip: -(으)ㄹ 뻔했다 with negative outcomes = relief ('Whew, I almost fell!'). With positive outcomes = regret ('I almost won, but didn't'). The key: the event did NOT happen.

examples.en: I almost got hit by a car

options: I almost failed the exam (but passed)
I will almost fail the exam
exercises.explanation: -(으)ㄹ 뻔했다 means it almost happened but didn't. So the speaker almost failed but in the end passed.

exercises.prompt: Does -(으)ㄹ 뻔했다 mean the event actually happened?
options: No — it almost happened but did NOT
exercises.explanation: -(으)ㄹ 뻔했다 always means the event was narrowly avoided — it did NOT actually happen. That's what makes it a 'near miss.'

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I almost cried'?
exercises.explanation: 울다 has stem 울 (ㄹ ending). ㄹ stems already end in ㄹ, so just add 뻔했다: 울 뻔했어요.

sections.title: Pretending: -는 척하다
sections.explanation: -는 척하다 means 'to pretend to' or 'to act as if.' The verb takes its modifier form before 척하다: present action verbs use -는 척하다, past uses -(으)ㄴ 척하다, descriptive verbs use -(으)ㄴ 척하다. 모르는 척하다 (to pretend not to know). 자는 척하다 (to pretend to be sleeping). 안 들은 척하다 (to pretend not to have heard). 바쁜 척하다 (to pretend to be busy). The pattern always implies that the reality is different from the pretense: 아픈 척했어요 (pretended to be sick — but wasn't really sick). 관심 없는 척했어요 (pretended to be uninterested — but was actually interested). 척 can also be written as 체: 모르는 체하다 = 모르는 척하다.
sections.tip: Match the modifier form to the tense/type: action verb present = -는 척, action verb past = -(으)ㄴ 척, descriptive verb = -(으)ㄴ 척. These are the same modifier rules as relative clauses.

examples.en: I pretended to sleep, but mom knew
I pretended to be fine, but actually I was sad
I pretended not to hear and walked past

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'pretend to be busy'?
exercises.explanation: 바쁘다 is a descriptive verb. Descriptive verbs use -(으)ㄴ for the present modifier: 바쁘 → 바쁜. So: 바쁜 척하다.

options: That person pretends to be rich
That person wants to be rich
exercises.explanation: 부자인 척하다 = pretend to be rich. 인 is the modifier form of 이다 (to be). The person is NOT actually rich but acts as if they are.

exercises.prompt: What modifier form goes before 척 for 먹다 (to eat) in present tense?
exercises.explanation: 먹다 is an action verb. For present tense, action verbs use -는 before 척: 먹는 척하다 (pretend to eat / pretend to be eating).
